sections.body: Specially marked spaces enforced 24 hours. Paid parking required Monday–Friday from 7am–6pm. For more information please contact TAPS at (209) 228-8277 or visit the TAPS website.

title: Research Server
subtitle: Modular research data storage facility (completed Fall 2019)
description: Modular research server facility providing electronic storage for UC Merced students’ and faculty research data. Located near the Central Plant and the Telecommunications Building.

bullets: Modular research server facility providing electronic storage for UC Merced students’ and faculty research data.
Located near the Central Plant and the Telecommunications Building.
Includes a containerized data center with supporting infrastructure and yard.
Academic program project completed in Fall 2019.

title: Facilities Services B
description: Drawn at 2:09:19 PM

title: Central Plant & Telecommunications Complex
description: Built in 2005, the Central Plant was the first energy plant in the U.S. to achieve LEED Gold certification and serves up to 2 million sq. ft. of campus space. The 90-foot Thermal Energy Storage (TES) tank allows chilled water to be produced at night to reduce energy costs and peak demand.

bullets: Heating, cooling, hot water, and steam for campus buildings
Electrical distribution and campuswide building controls
Thermal Energy Storage Tank (TES) for efficient chilled water
Telephone, internet, data processing, and emergency dispatch

title: University Police
subtitle: Mission, Vision & Values
description: UC Merced Police – Mission, Vision & Values

sections.body: In partnership with the community, enhance the quality of life through the highest standards of police services, violence prevention, emergency management, and community outreach so UC Merced can fulfill its missions of learning, research, teaching and public service.

To be innovative and proactive in our approach to safety, serving our constituencies with excellence through a diverse, well-trained, well-equipped organization capable of responding to today’s ever-changing environment.

sources.title: UC Merced Police Department – Mission, Vision and Values Statement

title: Social Sciences and Management Building
subtitle: Academic building
description: The Social Sciences and Management Building stands on 1.5 acres next to Science and Engineering 1. Groundbreaking took place in 2008 and the building opened in Fall 2011. The three-story, 101,900-square-foot building houses the School of Social Sciences, Humanities and Arts and meets high environmental standards.

Le Grand Lot Gold Zone

title: Student Services Building
subtitle: Classroom and instructional space
description: The Student Services Building is a two-building project consisting of a one-story pavilion lecture hall and a three-story steel-frame structure to provide additional classroom and instructional space. It is located on a small triangular parcel northeast of the Social Sciences and Management Building at the intersection of Ansel Adams Road and Scholars Lane, and is certified LEED Platinum.

bullets: Completion Date: Fall 2013 (pavilion) and January 2014 (three-story building)

sections.body: The building is certified as LEED Platinum.

title: Academic Office Annex (AOA)
subtitle: Academic support services
description: Services located in AOA 114 include the Office of Undergraduate Education (academic support) and the STEM Resource Center (tutoring and STEM support). Hours vary by tutor availability—see the linked sources for current schedules.

bullets: Office of Undergraduate Education – academic support (hours vary by tutor availability)
STEM Resource Center – tutoring and STEM support (hours vary by tutor availability)

title: Classroom and Office Building 1
subtitle: LEED Gold certified academic building (completed Jan 2006)
description: The LEED Gold certified Classroom and Office Building 1 was the second academic building in the University of California system to achieve a LEED certification.

bullets: Awards: LEED-NC Gold; 2007 UC/CSU/CC Sustainability Conference Best Practice Award for Best Overall Sustainable Design
Campus LEED Coordinators: Cynthia Hughes and Mark Maxwell
Architect & Engineers: Thomas Hacker Architects Inc, Taylor Engineering, LLC & The Engineering Enterprise

sections.body: UC/CSU/CCC Sustainability Best Practices Award: Outstanding Sustainable Design (2007)

bullets: Best energy performer on campus; saves 56% in energy costs over comparable facilities meeting only California building code requirements.
Energy-efficient HVAC: dual-fan/dual-duct variable air volume (VAV) system transfers unwanted heat to areas that need it; separate cooling/heating ducts improve efficiency.
Bicycle parking plus changing and shower facilities encourage travel without cars.
90% of construction waste recycled/diverted from landfill; wood waste chipped/reused/composted/used as biomass; concrete used as road base; metal recycled.
Indoor Air Quality Plan during construction; ducting/HVAC covered to prevent contamination; two-week ventilation flush-out prior to occupancy.
Sophisticated HVAC controls improve efficiency and provide feedback for tuning performance.

sources.title: UC Merced Design & Construction: Classroom and Office Building 1

title: Science and Engineering 1
subtitle: Academic & research building
description: Science and Engineering 1 provides laboratories, laboratory support space, teaching laboratories, and offices for the Schools of Natural Sciences and Engineering. The project achieved LEED-NC Gold.

bullets: Awards: LEED-NC Gold; 2009 UC/CSU/CC Sustainability Conference Best Practice Award; “Honorable Mention” for Best Overall Sustainable Design
Campus LEED Coordinators: Cynthia Hughes and Mark Maxwell
Architect & Engineers: EHDD Architects & Rutherford and Chekene
American Institute of Architects, San Francisco Chapter: Merit Award, Excellence in Architecture (2010)
International Institute for Sustainable Laboratories: Go Beyond Award, New Construction (2008)
UC/CSU/CCC Sustainability Best Practices Award: Outstanding Sustainable Design (2009)
Achieves water savings of 44% over a comparable building through automated faucets, low-flow laboratory fixtures, low-flow toilets, and waterless urinals.
93% of wood in the building comes from sources certified as responsibly-managed forests.
Building materials contain 48% recycled content (extra LEED point for exemplary performance).
43% of building materials were manufactured regionally (extra LEED point in exemplary performance).
Massive concrete structure helps temper Merced’s hot summer climate, improving energy performance by reducing cooling needs.
70% of construction waste was recycled and diverted from the local landfill; concrete waste was used as road base locally and metal was recycled.
Exterior sun shades and insulated low-e glass plank glazing reduce heat gain while providing natural light to reduce electricity use.

sources.title: UC Merced DC – Science and Engineering 1

title: Science and Engineering 2
subtitle: Research and instructional labs & core facilities
description: Science and Engineering 2 is a 102,000-square-foot facility overlooking the next phase of campus development. It provides research and instructional labs and core facilities, with significant outdoor spaces and collaboration areas. The building achieved LEED Platinum certification.

sections.body: Science and Engineering 2 is a 102,000-square-foot facility overlooking the next phase of campus development. It provides research and instructional labs and core facilities. Architectural features include several significant outdoor spaces, including a pedestrian plaza; a solar-panel shaded promontory above the canal that can serve as an event venue; and a porch adjacent to the engineering labs for outdoor learning opportunities. Breakout rooms with adjacent balconies provide collaboration space with vistas of the undeveloped landscape and future campus expansion. The building achieved LEED Platinum certification.

title: Mariposa
subtitle: Sierra Terraces Student Residences
description:
Sierra Terraces Student Residences

 

Overview

 

Sierra Terraces was UC Merced's second campus housing project and includes Tuolumne and Mariposa Halls, dormitory buildings that together house 400 students, primarily freshmen. Dormitory rooms can accommodate up to three students if necessary, making the total potential capacity of the building up to 600 beds. The buildings include lounges, meeting rooms/study spaces, and a kitchen in each building for student use. 

Project Details

Size: 84,500 square feet
Construction Cost: $24 million
Completion Date: August 2007
Awards: LEED-NC Gold
Campus Architects: E. James Smith and Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA
Project Director: Catherine Kniazewycz, AIA
LEED Coordinator: Mark Maxwell
Architect & Engineers: Fisher Friedman Associates, Taylor Engineering, and The Engineering Enterprise
Construction Managers: The Hallmark Group and ProWest Constructors

 

Sustainability Awards

American Society of Heating, Refrigerating, and Air-Conditioning Engineers: Technology Awards, Energy Savings Dorms (2010)

 

Project Highlights

  • The project has surpassed California’s Title-24 energy consumption standards by 42% and beats the LEED energy performance baseline by 44%,           achieving 9 points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance. 
  • 90% of construction waste was diverted from the landfill. 
  • Building materials had a combined value of 21% recycled content, earning 2 basic points plus a third point under Innovation and Design Credit for       Exemplary Performance. 
  • 49% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one basic point plus an additional point under Innovation and Design Credit for           Exemplary Performance.

bullets: Campus Architects: E. James Smith and Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA
Architect & Engineers: Fisher Friedman Associates, Taylor Engineering, and The Engineering Enterprise
Construction Managers: The Hallmark Group and ProWest Constructors

sections.body: American Society of Heating, Refrigerating, and Air-Conditioning Engineers: Technology Awards, Energy Savings Dorms (2010)

bullets: The project has surpassed California’s Title-24 energy consumption standards by 42% and beats the LEED energy performance baseline by 44%, achieving 9 points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance.
90% of construction waste was diverted from the landfill.
Building materials had a combined value of 21% recycled content, earning 2 basic points plus a third point under Innovation and Design Credit for Exemplary Performance.
49% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one basic point plus an additional point under Innovation and Design Credit for Exemplary Performance.

title: Kolligian Library
subtitle: Leo and Dottie Kolligian Library
description: The Leo and Dottie Kolligian Library consists of two concrete and glass wings housing the library and meeting rooms in the four-story east wing, and administrative offices and student services space in the three-story west wing. The wings are joined by a four-story “lantern” space with large public gathering areas. The LEED Gold building opened in 2005 and is a major hub of campus life.

bullets: Campus LEED Coordinators: Cynthia Hughes and Mark Maxwell
American Institute of Architects, California Council: Award of Merit, Savings by Design (2007)
American Institute of Architects, Sierra Valley: Design Award, Excellence in Architecture (2011)
The Chicago Athenaeum Museum of Architecture and Design: Green Good Design Award (2009)
UC/CSU/CCC Sustainability Best Practices Award: Overall Sustainable Design (2008)
Performs exceptionally, saving 51% in energy costs over comparable buildings meeting only California building code standards.
42% lower water consumption through automated faucets, low-flow toilets, and waterless urinals.
Carpet contains 37% recycled content; ceiling tiles contain 66% recycled content.
28% of materials manufactured regionally, reducing transportation energy costs.
Highly reflective “cool roof” reduces heat island effect.
CO2 monitoring controls ventilation to increase fresh air when occupied and reduce airflow when unoccupied.
Sophisticated HVAC and lighting controls monitor and modulate energy use and support performance evaluation.

title: Valley Terraces Student Residences
subtitle: Opened Fall 2005
description: Valley Terraces opened for UC Merced's first students in Fall 2005, providing housing for approximately 600 students in nine two-story apartment-style buildings with suites that include a common living room, double bedrooms, and shared restrooms for four to six students.

bullets: Campus LEED Coordinators: Cynthia Hughes and Mark Maxwell
Architect & Engineers: BAR Architects & Interiors; The Taylor Group Architects; Teter Consultants, LLP
Potable water consumption reduced by more than 50% via drought-tolerant landscaping, efficient irrigation, low-flow fixtures, and waterless urinals.
Bicycle parking provided throughout; showers available in the Terrace Center and Yablokoff-Wallace Dining Center.
HVAC, electrical, and lighting systems commissioned by a third-party team to verify efficient performance and occupant comfort.
91% of construction waste recycled instead of sent to landfill.
48% of building materials manufactured regionally, reducing transportation emissions and earning an extra LEED point for exemplary performance.
Concrete slabs contain 40% fly ash in place of cement.

title: Valley Terraces Student Residences
subtitle: Student housing complex (opened Aug 2005)

The complex includes the Terrace Center and the Yablokoff-Wallace Dining Center and received LEED Silver Certification in July 2007.

bullets: Campus LEED Coordinators: Cynthia Hughes and Mark Maxwell
Architect & Engineers: BAR Architects & Interiors; The Taylor Group Architects; Teter Consultants, LLP

title: Granite Pass Residence Hall
subtitle: Undergraduate student housing near Little Lake
description: Located just south of Little Lake, Granite Pass (Housing 1A/3B) provides additional undergraduate residential housing to UC Merced. The building was delivered in two phases, with the northern phase opening in Fall 2018 and the southern portion opening in Fall 2020.

bullets: Program: Undergraduate student housing, classrooms, and student life space

sections.body: The hall edges Little Lake with a pedestrian boardwalk and includes study rooms, lounge and meeting spaces, and sheltered bike storage. Ground-floor public spaces include classrooms and student life space connected to the pedestrian circulation network.

title: Sentinel Rock
subtitle: Residential facility near Little Lake
description: Sentinel Rock is among the newest residential facilities at UC Merced. It is located south of Little Lake and features 600 beds, typically available as triples and quads, with some single and double rooms available.

bullets: Also available: Some singles and doubles
Comfortable study and social spaces on each floor
A central study space on the first floor

sections.body: Singles, doubles, triples, quads.

sources.title: Video tour: El Portal and Sentinel Rock

title: Glacier Point Residence Hall
subtitle: Undergraduate housing, classrooms & student life space
description: Overlooking the new multipurpose Dining Facility, Glacier Point (Housing 1B) provides additional undergraduate residential housing to UC Merced. The six-story hall includes study rooms, lounges, meeting spaces, sheltered bike storage, and ground-floor public spaces connected to the pedestrian network.

bullets: Program: Undergraduate Student Housing, Classrooms and Student Life space

sections.body: A permanent name for the building was to be selected after opening. LEED Gold was noted as a design target in the project overview.

title: Sustainability Research & Engineering
subtitle: Lab 2A Wet/Dry Laboratory (Academic Quad)
description: A four-story teaching and research laboratory building (with partial basement) created as part of UC Merced’s 2020 Project campus expansion.

sections.body: Lab 2A is one of three teaching and research buildings anchoring the new Academic Quad as part of the 2020 Project campus expansion. The building has four floors above grade with a partial basement that links to the shaded loading dock. Wet/dry laboratories use modular planning to support future reconfiguration, with shared spaces (conference, break, colloquy) to foster cross-disciplinary interaction.

title: The Pavilion
subtitle: Multipurpose Dining Facility
description: A highly visible, 600-seat dining facility inspired by the Central Valley’s agricultural heritage, offering a variety of indoor/outdoor seating and supporting a healthy, sustainable food and community experience.

sections.body: The general seating area is conceived as an open space for engagement and relaxation, with a variety of indoor and outdoor seating options and smaller dining rooms. The dining area is oriented toward Little Lake to cultivate a relationship to the natural environment.
Additional storage is accommodated in the basement, which connects via a below-ground tunnel corridor to the Shared Service Loading Facility beneath the Academic Quad.

title: Arts & Computational Sciences
subtitle: Academic building (Building 2B)
description: This building creates a relationship between the Academic Quad on its northern side and the natural landscape to the south. Active program is clustered around activity nodes and interconnected outdoor spaces to ease wayfinding and foster impromptu meetings and collaboration for students, faculty and staff.

sections.body:
This building creates a relationship between the Academic Quad on its northern side and the natural landscape to the south.

Active program is clustered around activity nodes and interconnected outdoor spaces. These features ease wayfinding, and foster impromptu meetings and collaboration for students, faculty and staff. Exterior circulation articulates the pedestrian experience at the ground and upper levels facing the Academic Quad, helping all levels of the building participate in the campus experience.

title: Yablokoff Wallace Dining Center
description:
The Yablokoff Wallace Dining Center (YWDC) is the perfect complement to the Pavilion and provides a tasty variety of nutritious offerings in a modern space at the heart of campus. The YWDC is located on Scholars Lane just southwest of the Joseph E. Gallo Recreation & Wellness Center and near student housing. The YWDC welcomes campus residents, commuter students, staff, faculty and visitors.  

The YWDC accepts residential and non-residential meal plans, as well as Cat Dollars, credit cards and debit cards. Entry costs $11.95 for breakfast, $12.88 for lunch, and $13.95 for dinner.

Fall 2025 (Lunch, Dinner, Late Night)

Monday - Friday
Lunch: 10:30am - 2:00pm
Dinner: 3:00pm - 8:00pm
Late Night: 9:00pm - 12:00am

Saturday & Sunday: CLOSED

title: El Portal Residence Hall
subtitle: Student residence hall (LEED Platinum)
description: Housing 3D is a mixed-used student residence hall located at the gateway to most of UC Merced's campus expansion. The building will be 5 stories tall and at a minimum achieve LEED Gold certification.

title: Administration Building
subtitle: Enrollment Center / student services hub
description: Located at the gateway to Academic Walk, the Enrollment Center will be an integrated, one-stop location for students to conduct financial aid, registration, and other academic-related business with the University. It will also include additional classrooms, retail, and a campus store.

sections.body: Located at the gateway to Academic Walk, the Enrollment Center will be an integrated, one-stop location for students to conduct financial aid, registration and academic-related business with the University. It will also be the new site for additional classrooms, retail and a campus store.

title: Student Activities & Athletic Center
description:
Student Activities and Athletics Center

The Student Services and Athletics Center (formerly Recreation Center North) is sited adjacent to the Joseph E. Gallo Recreation Center. Located on a small site along Ranchers Road, the building adds approximately 19,000 gross square feet of activity space, offices with the potential for a future student wilderness center. The certification for the building is LEED Platinum.

Located on a transitional site between the Lake View Residential Neighborhood and the North Campus, the building strengthens a critical hub of student life and activity.  In design, full height glass walls connect the ground floor recreation space to an open plaza, thus creating an inviting indoor-outdoor connection.   The use of plaster, concrete and metal complements the existing Gallo Recreation Center, and the shed-like form of the building evokes simple but appreciated agricultural buildings seen throughout the San Joaquin Valley. 

Project Details

Construction Cost: $10 million
Completion Date: 2012

Awards: LEED Platinum

Campus Architect: Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA
Project Director: Neil Edwards
Design/Build Team: McCarthy Construction and WRNS Studio

bullets: Campus Architect: Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA
Design/Build Team: McCarthy Construction and WRNS Studio

sections.body: Formerly Recreation Center North; adjacent to the Joseph E. Gallo Recreation Center along Ranchers Road; approximately 19,000 gross square feet; LEED Platinum.

label.name: Joseph E. Gallo Recreation & Wellness Center

title: Joseph E. Gallo Recreation & Wellness Center
description:
Gallo Recreation and Wellness Center

The Joseph Edward Gallo Recreation and Wellness Center is the home of the campus recreation and health and wellness programs. It features a gymnasium with 500 bleacher seats and a full-size NCAA basketball court that can be configured in various ways to accommodate two cross-courts for basketball as well as volleyball and badminton courts. 

The recreation center also provides state-of-the-art cardio and fitness equipment, and a group exercise room with equipment.  The recreation and athletics program sponsors a number of club sports and activities such as hikes and whitewater rafting excursions.

The H. Rajender Reddy Student Health Center occupies the second floor and serves the health and wellness needs of the campus community, working with other campus departments and faculty to offer a range of wellness activities, from student peer health counseling and nutrition programs to massage therapy.

Project Details

Size: 35,400 square feet
Construction Cost: $9 million
Completion Date: August 2006
Awards: LEED-NC Gold
Campus Architects: E. James Smith and Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA
Project Director: Michael Chow, AIA
LEED Coordinator: Mark Maxwell
Architect & Engineers: Sasaki Associates, Taylor Engineering, and The Engineering Enterprise
Contractor: Howard S. Wright  Construction Company 

Building Highlights

  • This building has exceeded California’s Title-24 energy consumption standards by 44% and beats the LEED baseline performance standards by 49%, achieving all 10 possible LEED points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance.
     
  • This building achieves a water savings of 44% over a comparable building through the use of automated low-flow faucets, low-flow showerheads, low-flow toilets and waterless urinals, and drought-tolerant native landscaping.
     
  • Overall the building materials had a combined value of 15% recycled content, earning 2 LEED points.
     
  • 55% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one standard point and qualifying for a second point for Exemplary Performance.
     
  • Adhesives, sealants, paints and carpets used in the project have low volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and meet a number of low-VOC standards such as Green Seal, CRI Green Label, and SCAQMD Rule 1168.

bullets: Campus Architects: E. James Smith and Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA
Architect & Engineers: Sasaki Associates, Taylor Engineering, and The Engineering Enterprise
Contractor: Howard S. Wright Construction Company

Exceeded California Title-24 energy consumption standards by 44% and beat LEED baseline performance standards by 49%, achieving all 10 possible LEED points under credit EA 1: Optimize Energy Performance.
Achieves a water savings of 44% over a comparable building via automated low-flow faucets, low-flow showerheads, low-flow toilets and waterless urinals, and drought-tolerant native landscaping.
Building materials had a combined value of 15% recycled content, earning 2 LEED points.
55% of selected materials were manufactured regionally, earning one standard point and qualifying for a second point for Exemplary Performance.
Adhesives, sealants, paints and carpets used have low VOC emissions and meet low-VOC standards such as Green Seal, CRI Green Label, and SCAQMD Rule 1168.

title: Health & Aquatic Center
subtitle: Aquatic Center
description: The Competition Pool is an eight lane swimming pool suitable for intercollegiate competition. It is located adjacent to the Wellness Center, Transit Hub and Outdoor Recreation Fields and was completed in Fall 2020.

title: Welcome Center
description:
Visit UC Merced

Ways to experience campus:

  • Guided family and group walking tours with student ambassadors
  • Live virtual tours led by Welcome Center student ambassadors
  • Self-guided tours to explore campus at your own pace
  • Interactive and drone virtual tours for a bird’s-eye view

    • Visiting UC Merced is the best way to learn about our academic programs, housing, student life and research opportunities. Explore in-person and virtual options and plan your trip on the Visit Us page .

title: University Transit Center
subtitle: TAPS transit hub
description: Transportation and Parking Services (TAPS) transit hub serving students, faculty, staff, and visitors. The UTC offers bus shelters, seating, and real-time information about bus arrivals.

title: Recreation Field
subtitle: Outdoor Recreation Fields
description: The Outdoor Recreation Fields consist of new fields located adjacent to the competition soccer field and competition swimming pool. The fields will border Scholars Lane on the north and can be combined for large campus events.

title: Bobcat Field
description: Bobcat Field is reserved primarily for intercollegiate athletics practices and games. The field may be open for scheduled intramural activities and open recreation during posted hours. Use of the field outside of posted hours is not permitted.

bullets: Facilities may be closed during inclimate weather to protect the turf from damage.
Recreation & Athletics department staff is responsible for determining the open or closed status of each facility.
Use of the field is by reservation only through the Recreation & Athletics department.

title: Early Childhood Education Center
subtitle: ECEC
description: The Early Education and Childhood Education Facility (ECEC) is a LEED Gold modular facility providing child care to the campus and the community.

bullets: Campus Architect: Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA

sections.body: The single-story modular building provides indoor and outdoor programming for infants and pre-school students, including classrooms, a parent-child reading room, instructional space, and offices. Funding was provided by the Merced First 5 Commission, The David and Lucile Packard Foundation, the UC Office of the President, and gifts.

title: Greenhouse
subtitle: Academic research greenhouse
description: The Greenhouse is an approximately 2,500 sq ft research greenhouse adjacent to the Lab 3A building, supporting controlled plant-growth research in areas such as food and water security, biodiversity, climate change, renewable energy, and ecosystems.

sections.body: The building has been sited in a location well-suited for future expansion as funding becomes available.

title: EH&S Facility
description: The Office of Environment, Health & Safety (EH&S) provides a wide variety of services to faculty, students, and administrative staff to ensure that the campus operates in a safe and environmentally sound manner.

title: Classroom Office Building 2
description: Flexible classroom, academic support, research, and office space north of the Kolligian Library.

sections.body: The Classroom and Office Building 2 will provide approximately 51,000 assignable square feet of flexible classroom, academic support, research and office space. The site is north of the Kolligian Library.

title: Lab 3A: Biomedical Sciences and Physics
subtitle: Academic Quad teaching & research building (2020 Project)
description: Lab 3A is a five-story teaching and research building (with a partial basement) that anchors the new Academic Quad as part of UC Merced’s 2020 Project campus expansion initiative.

sections.body: Lab 3A is one of three teaching and research buildings anchoring the new Academic Quad for the 2020 Project expansion. The partial basement nestles into existing topography and links to the shared loading dock.

title: Tennis Courts
subtitle: Recreation & Athletics facility
description: Reservation-only tennis courts primarily for sport club practices, intramural activities, and open recreation. Courts may close during inclement weather to protect the surface.

bullets: Use outside of posted hours is not permitted.
May be closed during inclement weather to protect the surface.
Tennis use only; no non-tennis activities allowed on the courts.

sections.body: Tennis racquets and tennis balls may be checked out from the Joseph Edward Gallo Recreation Center with a valid Cat Card during normal Recreation Center hours.

title: Basketball Courts
description: Outdoor basketball courts are open dawn to dusk each day. Appropriate footwear is required (non-marking soles). Basketball courts are for athletic activities only.

bullets: Open dawn to dusk each day
May be reserved through Recreation & Athletics following the facility priority matrix standards

sections.body: Outdoor basketballs may be checked out from the Joseph Edward Gallo Recreation Center with a valid Cat Card by students and patrons during normal Recreation Center hours.

title: The Summits: Half Dome Student Residences
subtitle: Student residence hall (364 beds), completed Aug 2013
description: Located north of Cathedral and Tenaya Halls, Half Dome serves as a front door to Ranchers Road with views of Lake Yosemite and the Sierra Nevada. The 364-bed, five-story residence hall encloses UC Merced’s first housing courtyard and includes study rooms, lounges, computer terminals, laundry, and a kitchen.

sections.body: Located to the north of Cathedral and Tenaya Halls, Half Dome acts as a front door to Ranchers Road and provides unobstructed views of Lake Yosemite and the Sierra Nevada. LEED Platinum is the design target. The 364-bed residence hall stands five stories tall and encloses the edges of UC Merced's first housing courtyard. Similar to The Summits: Cathedral and Tenaya Halls, an average of five bedrooms are nestled near a common bathroom. Study rooms, lounge spaces, computer terminals, laundry, and a kitchen are available within the hall for residents to use. Ground floor public spaces have been programmed to energize the breezeways connected to the pedestrian circulation grid.

bullets: Campus Architect: Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA

description: Parking availability is reserved for specially marked spaces and enforced 24 hours. For more information or reservation questions, please contact TAPS at (209) 228-8277.

title: Library Lot 1
subtitle: Gold Zone parking
description: Parking availability: Faculty, Staff, Vendors. Marked spaces: ADA Parking, EV Charging Stations. Specially marked spaces enforced 24 hours. Paid parking required Monday–Friday from 7am–8pm. For more information contact TAPS at (209) 228-8277 or visit https://taps.ucmerced.edu/.

sections.body: Specially marked spaces enforced 24 hours. Paid parking required Monday–Friday from 7am–8pm.

title: The Summits: Cathedral and Tenaya Halls
description: Formerly known as "Housing 3", The Summits: Cathedral and Tenaya Halls provide up to 600 student beds to meet the on-campus housing needs of undergraduate students.

sections.body: Formerly known as "Housing 3", The Summits: Cathedral and Tenaya Halls provide up to 600 student beds to meet the on-campus housing needs of undergraduate students. The project includes two buildings with three floors of housing, two staff apartments and twelve resident advisor bedrooms. The buildings frame an internal courtyard and include study rooms, tutorial rooms and a space for a convenience store.

bullets: Campus Architect: Thomas E. Lollini, FAIA
American Institute of Architects, Sierra Valley: Design Award, Excellence in Architecture (2011)
Pacific Coast Builders Conference Award: Gold Nugget Award (2011)

The buildings frame an internal courtyard and include study rooms, tutorial rooms, and space for a convenience store.

title: Transportation & Parking Services
subtitle: Office hours, contact info, and mission
description:
Transportation & Parking Services

Office Hours

Monday through Thursday: 8:00am - 4:00pm
Friday: 8:00am - 12:00pm Remote: 1:00pm - 4:00pm
Closed for Lunch: 12:00pm - 1:00pm
Our office only accepts cash, check, money order or Cat Dollars.

Contact

UC Merced-TAPS
Facilities Services Building A
5200 North Lake Road
Merced, CA 95343

 

TAPS is located in Facilities Services Building A, behind the Science and Engineering buildings, on the corner of Mineral King Road and Facilities Loop.

TAPS

taps@ucmerced.edu

(209) 228-8277

Fleet Services

fleetservices@ucmerced.edu

(209) 746-9081

Event Services

tapsevents@ucmerced.edu

(209) 228-8277

Adjudication

citations@ucmerced.edu

(209) 228-2483

Suggestions and Feedback

To provide valuable feedback to TAPS regarding lot issues, road maintenance or CatTracks, please send us an email at jnardello@ucmerced.edu.

Our Mission

Transportation and Parking Services (TAPS) is committed to providing safe and reliable parking and transit services for all students, faculty, staff, and visitors. TAPS supports the mission of the University through a collaborative effort that emphasizes integrity, fairness, cooperation, professionalism, and respect.

bullets: Payment accepted: cash, check, money order, or Cat Dollars
